text: The surgeon locates the tattoo marking in the transverse colon, indicating the area of interest for the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon identifies a tattoo marking in the cecum, with visible ink spots located both proximally and distally to the intended area.

The surgeon has docked the robotic system and is preparing to perform an extended right hemicolectomy, focusing on the right side of the colon.

The surgeon begins isolating the vessels at the ileocolic pedicle, preparing for dissection in the extended right hemicolectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins isolating the vessels at the ileocolic pedicle, preparing for further dissection in the extended right hemicolectomy procedure.

The surgeon is positioned at the junction where the ileocolic vessels meet the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and vein (SMV). The vein is divided first, followed by the artery, with medial dissection being performed.

The surgeon is positioned at the junction where the ileocolic vessels meet the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and vein (SMV). The vein is divided first, followed by the artery, with medial dissection performed to separate the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon navigates up the superior mesenteric vessels towards the middle colic arteries, identifying the right branch of the middle colic. The mesenteric planned transection is marked to maintain orientation, with the surgeon delineating the distal margin across the middle colic area.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and proceeds with the mesenteric transection, locating the right colic pedicle.

The surgeon isolates the right colic artery and divides it, followed by the division of the right colic vein. The focus then shifts to the middle colic pedicle for further dissection.
The surgeon isolates the right colic artery and divides it, followed by the division of the right colic vein. The procedure then progresses to targeting the middle colic pedicle.
The surgeon isolates and divides the middle colic artery, which is visible in the surgical field, followed by the dissection and division of the middle colic vein.
The middle colic artery is isolated and divided, followed by the isolation and division of the middle colic vein, both clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon proceeds from the planned distal margin at the bowel wall, dissecting down to the mesentery and dividing the intervening mesentery along the marginal branches.
The surgeon proceeds from the planned distal margin at the bowel wall, dissecting down to the mesenteric area, and excises the intervening mesentery along with the marginal branches.
The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the tumor, revealing the underlying structures and preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the tumor, revealing the underlying structures and tissues.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization by first taking down the gastrocolic ligament, then moving around the hepatic flexure, and finally detaching the lateral attachments to the right colon and cecum.

The surgeon approaches from an inferior angle to complete the dissection of the lateral attachments of the specimen, ensuring all connections are severed.

The surgeon dissects the small bowel mesentery towards the planned proximal margin, preparing to check the perfusion of the bowel.

The surgeon staples the small bowel, then places the specimen down into the pelvis for later extraction through a Pfannenstiel incision.
The surgeon staples the small bowel and places the specimen into the pelvis, preparing it for extraction through a Pfannenstiel incision.
The surgeon aligns the isoparastaltic anastomosis, placing a stay suture on the far and near corners. A colotomy is made on the colon, followed by an enterotomy on the small bowel.
The surgeon lines up the isoparastaltic anastomosis, placing a stay suture on the far and near corners. A colotomy and enterotomy are made to facilitate the connection between the bowel segments.
The surgeon staples the bowel from the suprapubic port, which is the only incision larger than twelve millimeters.
The surgeon staples the tissue from the suprapubic port, noting that this will be the only incision larger than twelve millimeters.
The surgeon sews closed the common channel, beginning at the near corner and proceeding outward. A transition stitch is used, followed by a baseball stitch technique up to the far corner.

The surgeon begins to imbricate the tissue, folding and suturing it back down to secure the surgical site.
